title: Your first library endpoint
instruction: An HTTP handler is just a function that takes a request and writes a response. Run this — it creates a handler that responds with "Welcome to the Library API!".
successMessage: That's a working HTTP handler for your library. In production you'd register it with http.HandleFunc and serve it with http.ListenAndServe.

title: Writing a search response
instruction: Add a `searchHandler` that sets `Content-Type` to `text/plain` and responds with "Searching for: [query]" where the query comes from the URL parameter `q`. Call it with q="Go programming".

codeChecks.message: Get the query parameter: r.URL.Query().Get("q")
Write the response to w using fmt.Fprintf or fmt.Fprintln.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Query parameters are read with r.URL.Query().Get("q"). This is how your library search endpoint works.

title: Status codes — book availability
instruction: Use `w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)` for a 404 when a book is not found. The starter handles ISBN "978-0141036144" — add the 404 case for any other ISBN.
expectedOutput: Status: 200 | Body: Found book: 1984 by George Orwell

codeChecks.message: Write the 404 status: w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)
Write "book not found" in the body.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 200 for available books, 404 for missing ones. Always use http.StatusXxx constants — they are self-documenting.

title: Responding with JSON — book catalog
instruction: Add a second book to the catalog map (ISBN "978-0061120084"), then handle it in `bookHandler` and return JSON with `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(book)`.
hint: "978-0061120084": Book{ISBN: "978-0061120084", Title: "To Kill a Mockingbird", Author: "Harper Lee", Available: true}

codeChecks.message: Add a second book with key "978-0061120084" to the catalog map.
Replace the TODO comment with your book entry.

successMessage: JSON responses and 404 handling — this is a real library API pattern. json.NewEncoder(w).Encode writes directly to the response.

title: Multiple routes — library endpoints
instruction: Register multiple routes with `http.HandleFunc` or a `http.ServeMux`. Add a `/members` route that responds with "List of library members".
hint: mux.HandleFunc("/members", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { fmt.Fprintln(w, "List of library members") })

codeChecks.message: Register a /members route: mux.HandleFunc("/members", ...)
The /members handler should respond with "List of library members".
Replace the TODO comment with your route.

successMessage: Three routes, three handlers. http.NewServeMux() is Go's built-in router — for simple library APIs it is all you need.

title: Method checking — GET vs POST for books
instruction: REST APIs use different HTTP methods. Check `r.Method` with a switch — add `case http.MethodPost` that responds with "Adding a new book..." and status 201.
hint: case http.MethodPost:  w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated)  fmt.Fprintln(w, "Adding a new book...")

codeChecks.message: Add case http.MethodPost: to the switch.
Use w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated) for 201.
Respond with "Adding a new book..." for POST.
Replace the TODO comment with your POST case.

successMessage: GET lists books, POST adds books, DELETE gets 405. Method checking keeps your library API semantically correct.

title: Reading a request body — check out a book
instruction: Decode a POST body with `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&v)`. Always `defer r.Body.Close()`. Run this and change the member name from Alex to your own name.
hint: Change the body JSON from Alex to your own name.

codeChecks.message: Change the member_name in the body JSON to your own name.

successMessage: POST body decoded in two lines. json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req) handles everything.

title: Middleware — logging library requests
instruction: Middleware wraps a handler to run logic before and/or after it. Add a `timingMiddleware` that prints "Response sent" after calling the wrapped handler.
hint: func timingMiddleware(next http.HandlerFunc) http.HandlerFunc { return func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { next(w, r); fmt.Println("Response sent") } }

codeChecks.message: Define a timingMiddleware function.
Print "Response sent" after calling next.
Wrap the handler with timingMiddleware.
Replace the TODO comments with your middleware.

successMessage: The middleware chain: logging → timing → handler → timing finishes. Middlewares are composable — stack as many as you need.

title: Build a mini library REST API
instruction: Build a mini REST API for a library catalog. Define a `Book` struct, create a catalog slice, write `listHandler` and `addHandler`, route GET and POST. Test by listing, adding, and listing again.
hint: Use json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(books) to respond. Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&newBook) to read the body.

codeChecks.message: Define a Book struct.
Add JSON tags to the Book struct fields.
Define a listHandler function.
Define an addHandler function.
Check r.Method to route GET vs POST.
Use json.NewEncoder(w).Encode to respond with JSON.
Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode to read the POST body.
Return 201 Created for successful POST.

successMessage: A working library REST API — list books, add books, GET returns JSON, POST reads JSON. This is how every Go library API is structured in production.
